title: Marketing Consulting Services

evidence_facts.title: 2022 NAICS Manual
evidence_facts.quote: This U.S. industry comprises establishments primarily engaged in providing operating advice and assistance to businesses and other organizations on marketing issues, such as developing marketing objectives and policies, sales forecasting, new product developing and pricing, licensing and franchise planning, and marketing planning and strategy.

evidence_facts.title: 2022 NAICS Manual
evidence_facts.quote: Creating advertising campaigns and placing such advertising in media--are classified in Industry 541810, Advertising Agencies;

scope.population: Establishments creating advertising campaigns and placing advertising in media
scope.industry_mismatch: the fact identifies activity excluded from NAICS 541613 and classified to 541810

evidence_facts.title: The CMO Survey Firm and Industry Breakout Report – 2026

scope.population: Professional Services industry-sector respondents to the CMO Survey question on percentage of time using generative AI in marketing activities
scope.industry_mismatch: Professional Services marketing-leader sample is not a census of NAICS 541613 firms and measures client-company marketing activity rather than consulting-firm adoption

evidence_facts.title: Gartner Survey Reveals Marketing Leaders Expect AI Automation of Marketing Work to Double to 36% By 2028
evidence_facts.quote: Marketing leaders expect AI-driven automation of marketing work to more than double, from 16% in 2026 to 36% by 2028, according to a survey by Gartner, Inc, a business and technology insights company.

scope.unit: expected share of marketing work automated by AI
scope.period: 2026 and forecast 2028; survey conducted August through October 2025
scope.industry_mismatch: CMO survey across industries is not NAICS 541613 and measures expected work automation, not the share of firms materially adopting AI

evidence_facts.title: Small Business Check Up Survey: Economic Outlook, Business Performance, and Tax Policy — July 2025

scope.industry_mismatch: cross-industry small-business survey; it is not a NAICS 541613 or target-EBITDA-band owner estimate

evidence_facts.title: 2025 Digital Agency Industry Report
evidence_facts.quote: The overwhelming majority of agencies now identify as specialists (84%), signaling that broad-based generalist positioning is no longer viable.

scope.population: Digital agencies covered by Promethean Research
scope.unit: percent of agencies identifying as specialists
scope.industry_mismatch: Digital agencies include development, design, and marketing activities and are not limited to NAICS 541613 marketing consulting

evidence_facts.title: 2025 Digital Agency Industry Report
evidence_facts.quote: Most digital agencies use a mix of pricing methods. The most common is a mix of Time and materials, Fixed bid, and Retainer.

scope.population: Digital agencies covered by Promethean Research
scope.industry_mismatch: Digital agencies include activities outside NAICS 541613 and may include execution rather than advice-only consulting

evidence_facts.title: 2025 Digital Agency Industry Report
evidence_facts.quote: When agencies are small (<10 FTE), owners typically cover most of the core functions themselves.

scope.population: Small digital agencies with fewer than 10 full-time employees
scope.industry_mismatch: Digital-agency size segment is adjacent to, rather than a direct measure of, NAICS 541613 firms in the injected EBITDA target band

evidence_facts.title: 2025 Digital Agency Industry Report
evidence_facts.quote: Between 2010 and 2020, several new technologies emerged that massively impacted digital agencies.

scope.population: Digital agencies described by Promethean Research
scope.industry_mismatch: Digital agencies overlap marketing consulting but include development, design, and agency execution outside NAICS 541613

evidence_facts.title: Marketing Services | December 2024
evidence_facts.quote: M&A volume in the Marketing Services sector has reached 407 transactions announced or completed YTD.

scope.population: Marketing Services sector M&A transactions tracked by Capstone Partners
scope.industry_mismatch: Marketing Services sector includes digital marketing and other activities beyond NAICS 541613 and is not a count of distinct active consolidators

evidence_facts.title: Marketing Services | December 2024
evidence_facts.quote: M&A multiples in the Marketing Services sector have averaged a robust 3.5x EV/Revenue and 10.7x EV/EBITDA from 2021 through YTD, remaining on pace with the broader Business Services industry average of 2.3x EV/Revenue and 10.5x EV/EBITDA during the same period.

scope.population: Marketing Services sector M&A transactions used by Capstone Partners
scope.period: 2021 through year to date ended November 5, 2024
scope.industry_mismatch: Marketing Services sector and transaction-size mix are broader than NAICS 541613 and the injected $1M-$10M EBITDA band

evidence_facts.title: Service Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks - BizBuySell Report
evidence_facts.quote: Average Revenue Multiple Average Earnings Multiple 0.82 2.58

scope.population: 5,839 service businesses listed and sold on BizBuySell
scope.unit: average revenue multiple and average earnings multiple
scope.industry_mismatch: broad service-business data, mostly owner-operator scale, with earnings defined by the source as owner discretionary earnings rather than normalized EBITDA

role_judgments.rationale: Management-analysis work includes research synthesis, market analysis, and deck preparation that AI can accelerate, while client diagnosis and executive persuasion remain human-led.
Operations managers can automate reporting, scheduling, and internal coordination, but principal selling, judgment, and client accountability persist.
Market-research and marketing-specialist work has high exposure in research synthesis, segmentation drafts, content planning, and performance reporting, with validation and client context retained.
Proposal drafting and account preparation automate, but relationship selling and referral-driven business development remain less substitutable.
Status reporting, scheduling, follow-up, and deliverable tracking are automatable, whereas expectation management and escalation judgment remain human tasks.
Routine client-service triage, knowledge retrieval, and response drafting are highly exposed, while sensitive issue handling remains human-led.
The heterogeneous business-operations role includes repeatable analysis and coordination alongside judgmental specialist work, so a middle estimate is used.
Software developers are AI-augmented in coding and documentation, but architecture, integration, and data-governance work reduce the replaceable share.
Bookkeeping preparation, reconciliations, and routine reporting automate materially, while controls and professional accountability persist.
HR documentation, candidate screening support, and administrative workflows automate, but employee relations and compliance judgment remain human-led.

The unlisted wage share is likely to contain creative, content, analytics, and miscellaneous professional work; drafts and routine production are exposed but senior creative direction and client context remain.

scope_mismatches: F1 defines NAICS 541613 but does not provide task-level automation rates.
F3 measures client-company generative-AI use in a professional-services marketing-leader sample, not labor tasks in NAICS 541613 firms.
F4 measures expected cross-industry marketing-work automation, not occupation-level replacement in this industry.
F7 covers digital agencies, including execution activities outside advice-only NAICS 541613.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to identify the target work as marketing advice, planning, forecasting, pricing, and strategy.
Use F3 and F4 as directional evidence that marketing research, content, reporting, and automation work are AI-exposed.
Use F7 only to recognize that adjacent agency delivery is mixed across scoped projects, retainers, and time-based work.
Map each supplied 4-digit 5416 occupation to its likely task mix without relabeling the supplied SOC, and assign the residual to unlisted occupations.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select within-role fractions by task exposure while preserving human client judgment, persuasion, accountability, and relationship work; this judgment cannot be calculated from the cited facts.

pi_dist.rationale: Mixed time-and-materials, fixed-bid, and retainer pricing leaves material scope for clients to seek the AI efficiency benefit at renewal or project repricing; fixed fees permit some interim capture but do not remove client bargaining pressure.

scope_mismatches: F1 is an industry definition and does not report pricing or cost pass-through.
F7 covers digital agencies, not NAICS 541613, and reports pricing-method mix rather than the fraction of AI savings passed to customers.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to constrain the target to advice-oriented marketing consulting rather than media-placement activity.
Use F7 to establish an adjacent-market mix of time-and-materials, fixed-bid, and retainer arrangements.
Treat time-and-materials as most exposed to hours-based repricing, retainers as exposed at renewal and scope reset, and fixed bids as temporarily retaining delivery efficiency.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Weight those pricing mechanisms to select 0.65 as the expected fraction of operating-cost savings competed away or passed through; no source directly measures this value.

pi_moat.rationale: Vertical specialization and client trust can preserve some price capture, but the NAICS product is unlicensed advice and the adjacent agency evidence shows specialization rather than an exclusive legal or technical moat.

scope_mismatches: F1 defines the target industry but does not measure switching costs or moat strength.
F6 is digital-agency evidence rather than NAICS 541613 and reports specialization prevalence rather than price-capture durability.
bridge_steps: Use F1 to identify strategy, planning, and pricing advice as the target product.
Use F6 as directional evidence that adjacent firms often specialize, which can create vertical knowledge and client-context differentiation.
Recognize that specialization does not itself establish exclusivity, licensing, proprietary data ownership, or transferable client relationships.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Balance partial differentiation against an advice product that clients can benchmark, insource, or supplement with AI to select a 0.40 moat-retention fraction.

t50_years.rationale: The available evidence shows meaningful but not majority usage intensity and a two-year automation forecast; it does not report the target share of NAICS 541613 firms materially using AI, so the time to 50% is a forecast judgment.

scope_mismatches: F3 is a small professional-services marketing-leader sample and measures percentage of marketing activity, not firm-adoption incidence in NAICS 541613.
F4 is a cross-industry CMO forecast of marketing work automated, not a forecast of the share of NAICS 541613 firms materially adopting AI.
bridge_steps: Use F3 as a current directional signal that generative AI is used in a meaningful minority of professional-services marketing activity.
Use F4 as a directional two-year automation-growth signal.
Do not convert either work-share statistic mechanically into target-firm incidence; use them only to bound the adoption path.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Forecast that the target industry reaches 50% material firm adoption in 3.5 years, reflecting rapid tool diffusion offset by client-data, validation, and small-firm implementation variation.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: No fetched source measures material AI use among all U.S. NAICS 541613 firms. Professional-services marketing leaders report 23.08% mean time using generative AI, and Gartner reports 16% of marketing work automated; the selected firm-incidence estimate is deliberately not presented as either observed work-share figure.

scope_mismatches: F3 measures mean generative-AI use in marketing activities of professional-services marketing-leader respondents, not firm incidence in NAICS 541613.
F4 measures expected work automation among surveyed CMOs across industries, not current material firm adoption in NAICS 541613.
bridge_steps: Treat F3's 23.08% activity-use figure as evidence of active but incomplete use in an adjacent professional-services population.
Treat F4's 16% marketing-work automation figure as corroborating evidence that adoption has not yet made most work autonomous.
Define material adoption for this input as a firm using AI in at least one automatable consulting function, which is an incidence threshold not supplied by either source.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.35 as the current share of U.S. NAICS 541613 firms materially using AI; this is a judgmental population mapping, not an observed percentage.

historical_analogs.value: Digital-agency evolution from 2010 through 2020 is used only as a directional analog: successive technologies altered service mixes and created adaptation pressure, but it does not quantify AI adoption, price pass-through, or terminal demand for NAICS 541613.
historical_analogs.rationale: The analog supports disruption and service adaptation rather than a numerical forecast; generative AI may differ because it can affect both production cost and the advice product itself.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Directionally informative only; no numerical range is claimed

scope_mismatches: F9 concerns digital agencies, which overlap but are not identical to NAICS 541613 marketing consulting.
F9 describes 2010-2020 technologies and does not establish an AI-era causal outcome or rate.
bridge_steps: Use F9 only to establish that technology changes previously affected an adjacent agency market.
Do not transfer a historical adoption curve or financial effect from F9 to the target industry.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Characterize the history as a qualitative adaptation analog while withholding any numerical inference from it.

selection.rationale: The source reports 21% of surveyed small-business owners age 60 or older. It is used as a transparent adjacent-population anchor, not as a direct NAICS 541613 observation.

scope_mismatches: F5 is a cross-industry small-business-owner survey with geography not reported, rather than a U.S. NAICS 541613 owner census.
F5 does not isolate the injected $1M-$10M EBITDA target band or distinguish owner-operators from multi-owner firms.
bridge_steps: Use F5's reported 21% only as an age anchor for a fragmented, small-business-heavy adjacent population.
Do not adjust the figure mechanically for industry, firm size, or owner-count differences because the source supplies no such cross-tabs.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 0.21 for the target as a low-confidence proxy rather than asserting that the survey result directly observes NAICS 541613.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No fetched source directly documents a succession shortage for NAICS 541613 marketing consulting services.

scope_mismatches: none; no source fact is used
bridge_steps: Searched public web sources for NAICS 541613 marketing consulting succession, owner readiness, and marketing-agency succession-shortage evidence.
Located general owner-age and agency-operation material but no source directly documenting a succession shortage for the target NAICS population.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Treat the absence of located direct documentation as an estimate of false, not proof that no shortage exists.

active_consolidators.rationale: The source establishes an active marketing-services deal market, but transaction count is not a count of distinct consolidators and the target code is narrower. The selected count is a conservative estimate of distinct buyers plausibly active across the adjacent market.

scope_mismatches: F10 counts Marketing Services transactions rather than distinct consolidators.
F10 covers a broader sector and unspecified transaction-size and geography mix, not NAICS 541613 firms in the injected EBITDA band.
bridge_steps: Use F10 to establish that strategic and financial buyers are active in an adjacent marketing-services transaction market.
Avoid treating 407 transactions as 407 buyers because buyers can execute multiple transactions and many targets lie outside the target code.
Restrict the inferred buyer set to a small fraction of buyer organizations plausibly capable of acquiring or consolidating advice-oriented marketing firms.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 20 distinct active consolidators as a conservative buyer-count estimate; it is not directly observed in F10.

buy_mult.rationale: The selected entry multiple is below the broad marketing-services EV/EBITDA average and above broad small-service SDE evidence, reflecting the lack of target-band, post-market-owner-compensation comps and the need for a judgmental normalization.

scope_mismatches: F11 is a broad Marketing Services EV/EBITDA average with unspecified target-size mix, not a NAICS 541613 $1M-$10M EBITDA buy-side comp set.
F12 is broad U.S. service-business data and reports an earnings multiple that includes owner discretionary earnings, not EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation.
bridge_steps: Use F12's 2.58 average earnings multiple as a low-scale SDE anchor only, without equating SDE to EBITDA.
Use F11's 10.7x EV/EBITDA average as a larger-sector upper reference, without treating it as target-band pricing.
Bracket the target buy multiple between those non-comparable references after recognizing that a market-rate owner salary must be removed before an SDE comparison can approach EBITDA.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 4.5x EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation for a disciplined entry assumption; no fact supplies a mechanical SDE-to-EBITDA conversion or a target-band transaction multiple.

exit_mult.rationale: Exit potential is estimated above entry for a scaled specialist platform, but below the broad sector average because the source sector and deal sizes are wider than the target band and specialization does not guarantee durable price capture.

scope_mismatches: F6 measures specialization in digital agencies, not NAICS 541613 target-band companies or realized exit multiples.
F11 is a broad Marketing Services EV/EBITDA average with no disclosed match to the injected EBITDA band, NAICS 541613, or post-owner-compensation normalization.
bridge_steps: Use F6 as directional evidence that specialization can be present in an adjacent market.
Use F11's 10.7x broad-sector EV/EBITDA figure only as an upper reference for stronger, larger marketing-services assets.
Discount the upper reference for target-code, size, client-portability, and AI-exposure uncertainty.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Select 7.0x EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation as a possible scaled-specialist exit value; it is an underwriting estimate rather than an observed multiple.

pricing_structure.value: Mixed time-and-materials, fixed-bid/project, and retainer pricing; value- and performance-based pricing are minority mechanisms in the adjacent agency evidence.
pricing_structure.rationale: The adjacent digital-agency evidence identifies a mixed pricing stack. NAICS 541613 is advice-oriented and excludes media placement, so the exact mix cannot be observed from this source.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Time-based to fixed-scope and retainer mix; no target-code revenue shares observed

scope_mismatches: F7 covers digital agencies, including execution activities outside NAICS 541613, and does not report a NAICS 541613 revenue-weighted mix.
bridge_steps: Use F7's reported mix as the adjacent-market starting point.
Limit the target interpretation to advisory engagements, recognizing that the source may include implementation and media-related work that the target code excludes.
estimate_basis.judgment_step: Describe the target pricing structure as mixed rather than assign unsupported target-code revenue percentages.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: The role judgments indicate large exposure to AI-assisted production, while the adoption estimate remains below 50% and t50 is positive. Python supplies uplift_pp; the narrative does not treat current task exposure as immediate realized uplift.

terminal_value.justification: AI can reduce the operator's research, drafting, reporting, and planning costs, but client-side use can also substitute for portions of a generic advice product. Paid demand can persist for accountable senior judgment, client context, and specialized execution boundaries, while generic strategy and content-adjacent advice face product-replacement pressure.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: V and A use only task/adoption evidence and role judgments. Deal-volume, consolidator, and multiple evidence is confined to B and M inputs and is not used as a proxy for vulnerability or adoption.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Time-and-materials arrangements expose hours saved to immediate repricing; fixed bids can retain a temporary spread; retainers are exposed at renewal and scope reset. This is mapped to a high pi_dist estimate and only partial pi_moat retention rather than counted again in terminal value.

dataset_mismatch.description: The injected role mix is a 4-digit 5416 proxy rather than a 6-digit 541613 occupation mix. The injected labor share is W-2 based and may understate contractor-heavy delivery. Both values are retained unchanged as required; no silent adjustment is made.

sensitivity.impact: Would lower the role-weighted replaceable-share estimate because the unlisted 47.27% wage-share residual is large.
If contractor-heavy labor makes the W-2-based injected labor share understate total labor intensity, V and uplift sensitivity would be upward; the record does not alter the deterministic input.

subsegments.name: Marketing planning and strategy consulting
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Selected as dominant because the exact NAICS definition explicitly includes marketing planning and strategy; the source does not provide subsegment revenue shares.

subsegments.name: Sales management and forecasting consulting
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Included because the exact NAICS definition explicitly names sales forecasting and sales management consulting; revenue rank is a judgment because no fetched source reports target-code revenue shares.

subsegments.name: New product, pricing, licensing, and franchise planning consulting
subsegments.evidence_or_uncertainty: Included because the exact NAICS definition names new product development, pricing, licensing, and franchise planning; revenue rank is uncertain.

market_boundary.selection_basis: The exact NAICS definition establishes heterogeneous advisory activities but provides no revenue split. The score therefore follows the broad marketing-planning-and-strategy activity named in the definition, while excluding advertising-campaign placement, which Census classifies to NAICS 541810.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Directly changes A; no target-code firm-adoption time series was located.
Determines whether the t50 boundary remains positive and materially affects the adoption narrative.

top_uncertain_inputs.plausible_range: buy 3.0x-6.0x; exit 5.0x-9.0x EV/EBITDA after market-rate owner compensation
top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Changes M; available evidence mixes broad marketing-services EV/EBITDA with owner-discretionary-earnings service data.

reviewer_note: All selected numeric market and adoption inputs are estimates because the fetched evidence does not directly measure U.S. NAICS 541613 target-band firms. Adjacent digital-agency, marketing-services, CMO, and small-business evidence is disclosed with its population and normalization limits.
